utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality pitch decks that satisfy all 6 evaluation criteria. Both follow a problem-first narrative arc, use quantified outcome language, include urgency slides, provide buyer-type customization guidance, have presenter-usable slides with declarative headlines, and include ROI framing. The skill primarily helped enforce discipline in narrative structure and slide count, but the baseline agent without the skill also produced a strong output by reading the context files carefully. Overall, wi_skills demonstrates marginally better structural discipline and conciseness (10-12 slide arc vs. 14 slides), while wo_skills shows comparable depth on buyer customization and ROI framing. The gap between the two is smaller than anticipated — both are production-ready. wi_skills is slightly more effective due to tighter narrative discipline aligned to the prescribed framework.

utility_details.judge_error: No errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully with all 4 verifier checks passing.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective than wo_skills, with the clearest difference on J3 (dual-format objection doc usability). The wi_skills run explicitly produces two separate objection files — a scannable quick-reference table (quick-reference.md) optimized for mid-call lookup and a detailed prep playbook (detailed-playbook.md) — which is the key structural differentiator prescribed by the skill framework. The wo_skills run produces two battlecard files that are both full narrative documents with identical structure, lacking any scannable quick-reference format for live use. On all other criteria (J1, J2, J4, J5, J6), both runs perform comparably well — both include underlying concern diagnosis, specific proof points, timed demo scenes, embedded interaction checkpoints, and persona-specific guidance. However, the wi_skills output is generally more polished and comprehensive: the detailed-playbook includes a 'Default Framework' explanation, escalation language, and a pre-call prep checklist that add training depth beyond what the wo_skills battlecards provide. The skill framework's dual-format requirement is the most impactful differentiator and is only satisfied by wi_skills.

Fail: the output covers fewer than 4 components or merges them into a narrative that makes individual components hard to locate and use.

utility_details.judge_error: wo_skills failed verifier check V5 (email template content present), confirming the absence of email templates as a distinct asset. No other runtime errors detected in either run.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is clearly more effective. It produced a comprehensive 14-file enterprise sales enablement package that passes all 7 evaluation criteria, including structured persona cards in card format (J3), explicit playbook maintenance signals (J6), dedicated champion kit written for internal advocacy, persona-differentiated ROI framing with named storylines, and rich email template library. wo_skills produced a solid operational playbook covering most components but failed on two key criteria: (1) J3 - no dedicated persona cards in the required structured format with all four fields; (2) J6 - no maintenance guidance whatsoever. wo_skills also failed the V5 verifier check for missing email templates. The skill-guided run produced materially more actionable, structured, and complete output aligned with what new enterprise AEs would need to ramp independently.
